Residue Theory

In the second integral, make the change of variables $t=\pi-\theta, d t=-d \theta$, and $\mathbf{n}$ that $\sin \theta=\sin (\pi-t)=\sin t$. This transforms the integral into $-\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{0} e^{-s R \sin }$ which is the same as the first integral in (8); and so from (8) we get
$$
\left|\int_{\sigma_{R}} e^{i s z} f(z) d z\right| \leq 2 R M(R) \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} e^{-s R \sin \theta} d \theta
$$

At this point, we need an estimate on $\sin \theta$. On the interval $\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$, the grap $\sin \theta$ is concave down, because the second derivative is negative. Hence the $g$ of $\sin \theta$ for $0 \leq \theta \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$ is above the chordal line that joins any two points on graph. In particular, it is above the chord that joins the origin to the point ( $\frac{\pi}{2}$
$$
\leq \theta \leq \frac{\pi}{2} .
$$

\section*{LLARY 1}
whose equation is $y=\frac{2}{\pi} \theta$. This fact is expressed analytically by the inequalit.
$$
\sin \theta \geq \frac{2}{\pi} \theta, \quad 0 \leq \theta \leq \frac{\pi}{2},
$$
and is illustrated in Figure 4. From this inequality, it follows immediately $e^{-s R \sin \theta} \leq e^{-s R \frac{2}{\pi} \theta}$, and so from (9)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left|\int_{\sigma_{R}} e^{i s z} f(z) d z\right| & \leq 2 R M(R) \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} e^{-\frac{2 s R}{\pi} \theta} d \theta=-\left.2 R M(R) \frac{\pi}{2 s R} e^{-\frac{2 s R}{\pi} \theta}\right|_{0} ^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \\
& =\frac{\pi}{s} M(R)\left(1-e^{-s R}\right) \rightarrow 0, \text { as } R \rightarrow \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that an analog of Jordan's lemma will hold for $s<0$ if the cir $\operatorname{arc} \sigma_{R}$ is in the lower half-plane.

Applying Jordan's lemma in the special case when $f(z)$ is a rat function, we obtain the following useful result.
Let $f(z)=\frac{p(z)}{q(z)}$, where degree $q(z) \geq 1+$ degree $p(z)$, Let $\sigma_{R}$ deno semi-circular arc consisting of all $z=R e^{i \theta}$, where $0 \leq \theta \leq \pi$.
$$
\lim _{R \rightarrow \infty} \int_{\sigma_{R}} e^{i s z} f(z) d z=0, \text { for all } s>0
$$

Proof Let $M(R)$ denote the maximum of $|p(z) / q(z)|$ for $z$ on $\sigma_{R}$. Since degre $\geq 1+$ degree $p(z), M(R) \rightarrow 0$ as $R \rightarrow \infty$. Now apply Jordan's lemma.

We will next evaluate the improper integral
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x \sin x}{x^{2}+a^{2}} d x
$$

It is not difficult to show that this integral is convergent using integ by parts (Exercise 19). However, because the degree of $x^{2}+a^{2}$ is on more than the degree of $x$, the estimate in Step 3 of Example 1 will sufficient to show that the integral on the expanding semi-circle tend For this purpose, we will appeal to Jordan's lemma.
